members.name: scoped spawn/join (region-typed)
members.scenario_consumer: server request handling (worker threads over conc_queue); also compilers' parallel pipeline stages
members.contract_sketch: scope(|s| s.spawn(closure borrowing region-scoped &/&uniq data)) — spawn fails with err(EAGAIN) before the child ever runs; child trap = process abort; teardown: scope exit blocks until every child is joined, so no borrow outlives its region
members.platform_risk: default thread stack size differs (macOS ~512KiB vs Linux 8MiB) — spec one explicit default rather than inheriting the platform's; otherwise clone vs pthread_create is hidden behind the seal
members.trigger_if_deferred: n/a — kept; this is the root capability, unreachable from primitives (thread creation is a syscall)

members.name: unscoped/detached spawn
members.scenario_consumer: NONE
members.contract_sketch: would be: detached thread with 'static-only env, never joined, leaked at exit — exactly the teardown ambiguity scoped spawn exists to forbid
members.platform_risk: exit-time races with still-running detached threads behave differently under macOS vs glibc atexit/TLS teardown ordering
members.trigger_if_deferred: reinstate only if a v1 scenario exhibits a thread lifetime that provably cannot be nested in any enclosing scope (dynamic re-spawn after every candidate owner has returned); program-lifetime scope + conc_queue covers all 51 scenarios at par today

members.name: worker-pool knob: thread count + stack size
members.scenario_consumer: server request handling (pool sizing) and codec parallel validation (size = physical cores)
members.contract_sketch: pool(n, stack_bytes) -> pool | err; n=0 means default = available_parallelism (trusted sysconf/sysctl read, unreachable from primitives); teardown: pool drop drains queue then joins all workers
members.platform_risk: available_parallelism source differs (sysctl hw.* vs sched_getaffinity) and Apple Silicon P/E asymmetry makes 'core count' ambiguous — spec which count is returned on each platform
members.trigger_if_deferred: n/a — kept

members.name: worker-pool knobs: pinning/affinity/priorities
members.scenario_consumer: NONE in v1 (engines are the plausible future consumer)
members.contract_sketch: would be: pin worker i to cpu set / set sched class at pool construction; failure channel: err on EPERM/unsupported; no runtime re-pinning
members.platform_risk: HIGH and definitional: macOS has no hard affinity (thread_affinity_policy is a hint, ignored on Apple Silicon) so the API is untestable on the dev machine; SCHED_FIFO needs CAP_SYS_NICE on Linux — semantics materially diverge across the two platforms
members.trigger_if_deferred: a named engine or server scenario measures >=5% throughput or p99-latency improvement from pinning on the Linux x86-64 deployment target, with the benchmark preregistered before the API is designed

members.name: par.for_chunks
members.scenario_consumer: codecs: parallel validation and the partition pass of parallel sort
members.contract_sketch: split &uniq [T] into disjoint chunks with checker-proved disjointness carried across the thread boundary (the nonforgeable part users cannot build from primitives); body runs per chunk on the pool; returns only after all chunks complete; any trap aborts the process; no early-exit channel in v1
members.platform_risk: none material; chunk-size heuristics may want cache-line awareness but that is tuning, not semantics
members.trigger_if_deferred: n/a — kept; the borrow-carrying disjointness proof across threads is exactly the fact channel users must not be able to forge

members.name: par fork-join (recursive task spawn)
members.scenario_consumer: build-system-style fork-join (compilers) and recursive parallel sort (codecs)
members.contract_sketch: inside a par scope: h = s.fork(task); v = h.join() — join returns the task's value; work-stealing is internal; trap in any task aborts the process; teardown: par scope exit implies every forked task has completed
members.platform_risk: correctness rests on the internal deque's memory orderings — arm64's weak model (dev machine) is the honest stress bed; x86-64 TSO can mask fence bugs, so passing-on-deployment-target is not sufficient evidence
members.trigger_if_deferred: n/a — kept

members.name: par.reduce (wrapping-int monoids only)
members.scenario_consumer: codec checksums (adler/CRC-style sums) — served by the merged form
members.contract_sketch: ship as a checked-library helper over par.for_chunks: each chunk writes its partial into a preallocated &uniq slot, then a serial O(chunks) fold; wrapping-int monoids make the combine order-insensitive, so this is at par with a fused builtin; zero new trusted LOC
members.platform_risk: none — inherits for_chunks
members.trigger_if_deferred: promote to sealed builtin only if a preregistered codec checksum benchmark shows the library form losing >=3% to a fused reduce

members.name: thread-local storage
members.scenario_consumer: NONE user-visible (allocator TLS is runtime-internal, not a member)
members.contract_sketch: replacement: worker-index-addressed arrays (see thread id/worker index row) — one indexed load vs a TLS-register load, at par; avoids the TLS destructor-ordering soundness surface entirely
members.platform_risk: the cut avoids real divergence: TLS destructor ordering and dlopen interaction differ between macOS dyld and glibc — a large verification cost for a sealed TLS row
members.trigger_if_deferred: reinstate if a v1 scenario measures >=5% overhead from worker-indexed state vs native TLS, or if detached spawn is ever reinstated (fixed-pool indexing breaks without a fixed pool)

members.name: sleep
members.scenario_consumer: server request handling: retry/backoff and timed polling loops
members.contract_sketch: sleep(ns) blocks the calling thread for at least ns; EINTR retried internally; no error channel; no teardown interaction. Note: if the ratified condvar surface includes timed wait, demote this row to library sugar over it (MERGE) — sleep is never performance-sensitive
members.platform_risk: macOS timer coalescing can overshoot substantially — spec a minimum-bound-only contract, never an accuracy bound
members.trigger_if_deferred: n/a — kept (a raw nanosleep syscall is unreachable from primitives unless condvar timed-wait already ships)

members.name: yield
members.scenario_consumer: NONE — spin backoff lives inside the sealed mutex and internal deque, not in user code
members.contract_sketch: would be sched_yield, whose semantics are notoriously unspecified; exposing it invites user spin loops that compete with the kept sync members
members.platform_risk: sched_yield behavior differs across kernels/schedulers — precisely why it should stay internal
members.trigger_if_deferred: reinstate only if a scenario demonstrates a user-level wait loop beating the kept sync primitives by >=5% and needing yield for fairness — expected never

members.name: thread id / pool worker index
members.scenario_consumer: servers and engines: per-worker state via worker-indexed arrays (this row is what makes the TLS cut viable)
members.contract_sketch: worker_index() -> usize < pool size, stable for the worker's lifetime; thread id = opaque u64 unique for process lifetime (synthesized, not gettid); no failure channel; ~trivial trusted LOC
members.platform_risk: none — ids are synthesized in the runtime, not read from the OS
members.trigger_if_deferred: n/a — kept

members.name: thread naming
members.scenario_consumer: NONE (debuggability nicety, no performance claim)
members.contract_sketch: would be: set_name at spawn, best-effort, truncated, no error channel
members.platform_risk: pthread_setname_np diverges: macOS is self-only 1-arg, Linux is 2-arg with a 15-byte cap — a genuinely different contract per platform for zero scenario value
members.trigger_if_deferred: first debugging session against the Linux deployment target where anonymous threads materially slow diagnosis; reinstatement cost then is ~20 trusted LOC

members.name: panic-across-thread semantics
members.scenario_consumer: every concurrent scenario (semantic rule, not a member; zero new trusted LOC)
members.contract_sketch: ratified: trap on any thread = immediate whole-process abort; no cross-thread unwinding, no lock poisoning, no observation channel — join never reports 'child trapped' because the process is already gone
members.platform_risk: none — abort delivery is identical on both platforms
members.trigger_if_deferred: n/a — already ruled (trap = abort, provisional)

members.name: scheduler internals (Chase-Lev deque)
members.scenario_consumer: internal-only: implements par fork-join and for_chunks scheduling; never user-visible, so no D16 sealed-member row is spent on it
members.contract_sketch: per-worker deque with owner push/pop and thief steal; no public API; teardown: pool shutdown drains queues before joining workers; must carry its own memory-ordering proof through the acceptance battery since it is trusted LOC
members.platform_risk: the classic Chase-Lev fence bugs are invisible on x86-64 TSO and manifest on arm64 — run the adversarial concurrency tests on the arm64 dev machine as the primary bed, not just the deployment target
members.trigger_if_deferred: n/a — kept internal; exposing it publicly would require its own D16 case, which nothing demands

members.name: blocking-call integration (escape hatch for IO in par bodies)
members.scenario_consumer: NONE in v1 — card rule stands: no blocking calls in par bodies; blocking IO belongs on scoped threads feeding the pool via conc_queue
members.contract_sketch: would be: block_in_place-style hatch that retires the current worker and spawns a replacement so the pool keeps its width; failure channel: err on spawn failure; teardown: replacement joins at pool drop
members.platform_risk: none material; the hatch itself is platform-neutral
members.trigger_if_deferred: the build-system fork-join scenario (compilers, per-task file IO) shows >=10% end-to-end throughput loss or structural impossibility restructuring IO onto dedicated scoped IO threads vs an in-body hatch, measured on the deployment target

members.name: barrier/latch primitives
members.scenario_consumer: NONE as sealed members — engine frame sync is served by a checked-library barrier over the kept counter+mutex+condvar at par (barrier waits in the named scenarios are microsecond-to-millisecond scale, not futex-shaving territory)
members.contract_sketch: library form: latch = counter with a condvar wake at zero; barrier = two-phase latch; all built from already-ratified sync members, zero new trusted LOC
members.platform_risk: none — inherits the sync members' already-adjudicated futex/ulock divergence
members.trigger_if_deferred: reinstate as sealed if a preregistered engine scenario measures >=5% frame-time regression of the library barrier vs a native futex barrier on the deployment target

members.name: scope cancellation
members.scenario_consumer: NONE — server request-timeout is served by cooperative flag polling via publish-snapshot-cell in loop bodies, at par and with zero trusted LOC
members.contract_sketch: cooperative form (library): shared cancel flag checked at chunk/task boundaries; forced cancellation of a blocked thread is not expressible without IO-interruption machinery, which is the deferred evring's territory
members.platform_risk: forced cancellation would hit pthread_cancel/signal divergence (macOS vs Linux cancellation points) — the cut avoids an entire cross-platform semantics swamp
members.trigger_if_deferred: rides the evring reinstatement trigger: if a server scenario requires cancelling threads blocked in IO within a scope, adjudicate cancellation together with evring, not before

members.name: tcp_connect
members.scenario_consumer: network sockets: TCP/UDP request paths (client leg: DB clients, servers dialing upstreams)
members.contract_sketch: socket()+connect() merged: blocking connect to (ip,port) -> linear tcp_stream or errno-class error (ECONNREFUSED/ETIMEDOUT/ENETUNREACH); OS-default SYN timeout (caller deadline deferred with readiness); macOS SO_NOSIGPIPE set at creation; teardown = close via handle drop.
members.platform_risk: Low. SO_NOSIGPIPE exists only on Darwin (Linux path suppresses SIGPIPE per-send instead); SOCK_CLOEXEC atomic on Linux, fcntl race-window on macOS (dev-only exposure).
members.trigger_if_deferred: N/A — kept.

members.name: tcp_listen
members.scenario_consumer: network sockets: TCP/UDP request paths (server accept side; the 51-map server scenarios)
members.contract_sketch: socket()+bind()+listen() merged: (ip,port,backlog,reuseaddr:bool) -> linear tcp_listener or errno error (EADDRINUSE/EACCES); teardown = close, which unblocks no one (accepting threads get EBADF-free ECONNABORTED contract via shutdown-then-close ordering).
members.platform_risk: Low. Backlog clamping differs (somaxconn defaults); SO_REUSEADDR semantics on macOS are slightly laxer than Linux — contract states Linux (deploy-target) semantics as normative.
members.trigger_if_deferred: N/A — kept.

members.name: tcp_accept
members.scenario_consumer: network sockets: TCP/UDP request paths — thread-per-connection server loop is the v1 server architecture with evring deferred
members.contract_sketch: blocking accept on shared<tcp_listener> -> (linear tcp_stream, peer_addr) or errno error; ECONNABORTED retried internally, EMFILE/ENFILE surfaced; per-stream teardown independent of listener.
members.platform_risk: Low. accept4(SOCK_CLOEXEC|inherit-nonblock=off) on Linux vs accept+fcntl on macOS; Darwin-inherited O_NONBLOCK from listener quirk hidden inside the row.
members.trigger_if_deferred: N/A — kept.

members.name: socket_raw / bind_raw / listen_raw (standalone rows)
members.scenario_consumer: NONE standalone — every named consumer wants the composed handle, never a bare unbound socket
members.contract_sketch: Folded into tcp_connect, tcp_listen, udp_bind; a bare fd with no role has no checked contract and no consumer, so it never exists as a value.
members.platform_risk: None (removed surface).
members.trigger_if_deferred: Reinstate a raw socket row only if a kept scenario needs a socket type outside {TCP, UDP} (e.g. unix-domain sockets get admitted); then it enters as its own composed row, not raw socket().

members.name: net_recv (stream read)
members.scenario_consumer: network sockets: TCP/UDP request paths (request parsing in thread-per-conn servers)
members.contract_sketch: Merge into the kept io-file read row: blocking read on fd-backed byte stream -> 0..n bytes, 0 = EOF/peer-FIN, EINTR per the io row's stated policy, ECONNRESET as errno error; teardown via stream close. One read row, one contract, two handle kinds.
members.platform_risk: Low — read(2) semantics are the portable part of sockets; only the errno set widens (ECONNRESET/ETIMEDOUT when SO_RCVTIMEO fires -> EAGAIN mapped to a timeout error value).
members.trigger_if_deferred: Split back into a dedicated net row only if the io read row's verified contract (EINTR/short-read policy) proves incompatible with the SO_RCVTIMEO timeout-error channel during the acceptance battery.

members.name: net_send (stream write)
members.scenario_consumer: network sockets: TCP/UDP request paths (response emission)
members.contract_sketch: Distinct from io write, NOT merged: send with MSG_NOSIGNAL (Linux) / write on SO_NOSIGPIPE socket (macOS); returns n<=len partial-write count; peer-gone is an EPIPE/ECONNRESET error value, never a signal (trap=abort world cannot tolerate SIGPIPE process death); teardown = shutdown(WR)+close.
members.platform_risk: MATERIAL — this is why it is not merged with file write: SIGPIPE suppression is MSG_NOSIGNAL per-call on Linux but socket-option-at-creation on Darwin; the row hides the divergence, a merged write row could not.
members.trigger_if_deferred: N/A — kept.

members.name: net_sendv (writev/sendmsg gather)
members.scenario_consumer: network sockets: TCP/UDP request paths — header+body response assembly; par is unreachable without it (user choice is an extra copy or two syscalls interacting with Nagle)
members.contract_sketch: gather-send of iovec list with same SIGPIPE-suppression and partial-write contract as net_send (returns total bytes consumed across iovecs); errno error channel identical; teardown shared with stream.
members.platform_risk: Low. IOV_MAX differs (1024 both in practice); Linux uses sendmsg+MSG_NOSIGNAL, Darwin plain writev on NOSIGPIPE socket.
members.trigger_if_deferred: N/A — kept. (readv counterpart stays out: no consumer demonstrated a par gap on scattered receive; trigger = a codec/server scenario showing a measured copy on receive path >5% of request cost.)

members.name: net_shutdown
members.scenario_consumer: network sockets: TCP/UDP request paths — half-close for protocol EOF, and the blocking-world cancellation primitive: shutdown(RD) from a sibling thread is the only way to release a thread parked in blocking read once evring is deferred
members.contract_sketch: shutdown(how in {RD,WR,RDWR}) on shared<tcp_stream>; releases blocked readers/writers with EOF/EPIPE; ENOTCONN after peer-close is defined as success; does not free the fd — teardown remains close.
members.platform_risk: Moderate flag: waking a thread blocked in read via shutdown is reliable on both kernels, but macOS wakes with 0/EOF where Linux may also surface ENOTCONN on the racing call — contract normalizes both to EOF.
members.trigger_if_deferred: N/A — kept.

members.name: net_close
members.scenario_consumer: all socket consumers
members.contract_sketch: Merge into the kept io close/handle-drop row: linear-handle consumption, close(2) once, EINTR-on-close policy inherited from the io row (fd is dead either way); no socket-specific behavior (lingering close semantics deliberately not exposed — SO_LINGER excluded).
members.platform_risk: Low; EINTR-on-close divergence already owned by the io close row's verified contract.
members.trigger_if_deferred: Split out only if a scenario demands graceful-close-with-drain (SO_LINGER-class) semantics; trigger = a kept protocol scenario whose correctness (not just politeness) requires drained close.

members.name: sockopt_set (closed enum: TCP_NODELAY, SO_REUSEADDR, SO_RCVBUF, SO_SNDBUF, SO_RCVTIMEO, SO_SNDTIMEO)
members.scenario_consumer: network sockets: TCP/UDP request paths — NODELAY for request/response latency par (Nagle costs ~40ms), REUSEADDR for server restart, buf sizes for throughput par, RCV/SND timeouts are load-bearing: blocking thread-per-conn without read deadlines lets one dead peer pin a thread forever
members.contract_sketch: typed setter per enum member on the socket handle (no generic setsockopt, no getsockopt in v1); failure = errno error; values clamped by kernel are accepted silently per option contract; no teardown. Excluded by name: SO_REUSEPORT, SO_KEEPALIVE, SO_LINGER, TCP_CORK/NOPUSH.
members.platform_risk: MATERIAL on buffers: Linux doubles the requested SO_RCVBUF/SNDBUF and clamps to rmem_max/wmem_max, Darwin does not — contract must say 'hint, kernel-clamped', never 'exact'. Timeouts and NODELAY portable.
members.trigger_if_deferred: For each excluded option: SO_REUSEPORT when a single accept thread is measured saturated (<par accept rate) in a kept server scenario; SO_KEEPALIVE when a kept scenario has long-lived idle connections dying undetected past the SO_RCVTIMEO envelope; SO_LINGER per net_close trigger.

members.name: readiness_wait (kqueue/epoll: wait-readable/writable on a set)
members.scenario_consumer: readiness/completion multiplexing (the demand-map scenario) — but that scenario is already the consumer of the deferred evring row; v1 server scenarios are served by thread-per-connection
members.contract_sketch: If reinstated: register fd set, blocking wait -> ready subset with level-triggered semantics only; failure = errno; teardown = deregister-on-close, defined against the close row. Not built now: thread-per-conn + blocking IO + SO_RCVTIMEO + shutdown-as-cancel is par for v1 servers up to ~c10k on the Linux deploy target (~8KB kernel + ~32KB touched user stack ≈ 0.5 GB RSS at 10k, O(1) scheduler); the par line breaks in the c10k–c50k band on RSS and wakeup latency, and no v1 scenario names >10k concurrent connections per process.
members.platform_risk: HIGH and a reason to defer: kqueue vs epoll differ in registration model, edge/level defaults, error reporting on closed fds, and EVFILT granularity — a portable sealed contract is real design work that would be paid twice if evring later subsumes it.
members.trigger_if_deferred: Fires when (a) a named demand-map scenario requires >10,000 concurrent mostly-idle connections per process, or (b) thread-per-conn measured at a kept scenario's concurrency is >1.3x worse p99 latency or >2x RSS than an epoll reference on Linux x86-64. On firing, first re-evaluate un-deferring evring (completion model, one surface) — add this readiness row only if the evring trigger has independently not fired.

members.name: dns_resolve (getaddrinfo)
members.scenario_consumer: NONE at v1 — request-path scenarios connect to config-provided endpoints; no kept scenario requires runtime hostname resolution
members.contract_sketch: Deferred with a 'resolve via OS card': v1 programs take numeric ip:port (address parsing is pure checked code, not a sealed row); the card documents resolving at deploy/config time (getent hosts / dscacheutil). Rationale: getaddrinfo drags NSS/dlopen/mDNSResponder into the trusted surface, blocks unboundedly with no deadline, and its five-part battery cost is the worst in the unit.
members.platform_risk: HIGH and part of why it defers: macOS resolves through mDNSResponder (.local, ordering quirks), Linux through nsswitch/resolv.conf — same call, materially different resolution behavior between dev and deploy machines.
members.trigger_if_deferred: Fires when a kept scenario must resolve names at runtime — concretely, an outbound HTTP/RPC client scenario is admitted to the demand map, or a kept DB-client scenario's endpoints demonstrably cannot be fixed as numeric addresses in config. Reinstate as a sealed blocking getaddrinfo row with an explicit no-deadline caveat, or as a checked-library stub resolver over the kept UDP rows if par allows.

members.name: tls
members.scenario_consumer: NONE — no v1 demand-map scenario names TLS, and it fails D16 on the other prong too
members.contract_sketch: Out of the net unit, stated explicitly: TLS is pure computation layered over the kept stream rows, so ordinary users CAN implement it from primitives + checked libraries — it is a future checked library, never a sealed row; keeping it would be the single largest trusted-code liability in the stdlib for zero named consumers.
members.platform_risk: N/A (no row). Note: 'checked-library TLS at par' is a CPU-intrinsics question (AES/SHA instructions), which files against a future intrinsics unit, not net.
members.trigger_if_deferred: N/A — cut, not deferred. Reopen only as a checked library when a scenario names it; a sealed row would additionally require evidence that checked-library crypto cannot reach par even with the intrinsics unit resolved.

members.name: udp_bind
members.scenario_consumer: network sockets: TCP/UDP request paths (UDP leg: engines' realtime channels, telemetry, DNS-shaped services in the demand map)
members.contract_sketch: socket(DGRAM)+bind merged: (ip,port; port 0 = ephemeral) -> linear udp_socket or errno error; optional connect(peer) variant for fixed-peer filtering + ECONNREFUSED delivery; teardown = close via handle drop.
members.platform_risk: Low; ICMP-error surfacing (ECONNREFUSED on connected UDP) is reliable on Linux, best-effort on Darwin — contract marks it advisory.
members.trigger_if_deferred: N/A — kept.

members.name: udp_sendto
members.scenario_consumer: network sockets: TCP/UDP request paths (UDP leg, as above)
members.contract_sketch: blocking sendto(buf, addr): whole datagram sent or errno error (EMSGSIZE for oversize, EAGAIN when SO_SNDTIMEO fires) — never partial, by datagram semantics; no SIGPIPE channel; teardown shared with udp_socket.
members.platform_risk: Low; max datagram size and default sndbuf differ across the two kernels (contract exposes EMSGSIZE rather than a constant).
members.trigger_if_deferred: N/A — kept.

members.name: udp_recvfrom
members.scenario_consumer: network sockets: TCP/UDP request paths (UDP leg, as above)
members.contract_sketch: blocking recvfrom(buf) -> (n, peer_addr, truncated: bool) or errno error (EAGAIN on SO_RCVTIMEO); a datagram longer than buf is delivered truncated with the flag set and the remainder discarded — the flag is the portable contract; teardown shared with udp_socket.
members.platform_risk: MATERIAL, hence the bool-only contract: Linux MSG_TRUNC can report the true original datagram length, Darwin only flags truncation — the row exposes the intersection (flag, not length) so behavior is identical on dev and deploy.
members.trigger_if_deferred: N/A — kept. (Exposing true-length-on-truncation reinstates only if a kept codec/protocol scenario needs it, accepting a Linux-only contract note.)

members.name: filemap.map_file_ro
members.scenario_consumer: read-a-big-index (database reading a large immutable index/SSTable; compiler reading large object/archive inputs)
members.contract_sketch: map(io_file_handle, offset, len) -> RoMap owning a fixed-length read-only byte view; ONLY the length is a trusted fact (fixed at map time); mapped bytes are never margined and every content-derived check on them is non-elidable (external file mutation can change bytes but never violates the length bound, so no fact forgery); fails with an error result at map time (bad handle/offset/ENOMEM); touching a page truncated away externally is SIGBUS -> process abort (consistent with trap=abort, documented); teardown = munmap on drop of RoMap, infallible.
members.platform_risk: Page size 16KB on macOS arm64 vs 4KB on Linux x86-64 — offset/len rounding must be page-size-parametric, never hardcoded 4K; SIGBUS-on-truncation aborts on both but is easier to hit under macOS APFS clone semantics differences; interaction with O_DIRECT-opened handles differs (Linux allows mmap of O_DIRECT fd, semantics murky) — restrict map to non-O_DIRECT handles.
members.trigger_if_deferred: N/A (kept)

members.name: filemap.unmap
members.scenario_consumer: same as map_file_ro
members.contract_sketch: Not a separate row: unmap IS the teardown of RoMap (drop = munmap, infallible, no error channel); no user-visible early-unmap-while-borrowed possible because the byte view borrows the RoMap.
members.platform_risk: None beyond map row.
members.trigger_if_deferred: N/A (merged into map_file_ro)

members.name: filemap.madvise_subset
members.scenario_consumer: NONE (pure perf hint; read-a-big-index is functionally complete without it)
members.contract_sketch: Would be: advise(RoMap, range, {sequential|willneed|dontneed-cold}) hint-only, no semantic effect, infallible (errors swallowed), no teardown — deferring is safe precisely because it cannot change observable behavior.
members.platform_risk: High divergence: macOS lacks/reinterprets several Linux advices (MADV_FREE vs MADV_DONTNEED behavior differs materially); a hint tuned on Apple Silicon dev boxes may be a no-op or pessimization on the Linux x86-64 deployment target — another reason to defer until measured there.
members.trigger_if_deferred: Pinned read-a-big-index benchmark on Linux x86-64 shows >=10% wall-time or >=20% major-fault improvement from MADV_SEQUENTIAL/WILLNEED over default readahead; reinstate only the advice values that cleared the bar.

members.name: filemap.map_file_private_rw
members.scenario_consumer: NONE named in v1 (nearest candidate: linker patch-in-place, but pread-into-owned-buffer is the par baseline)
members.contract_sketch: Would be: COW private writable mapping; hazard: not-yet-written pages still track the file, so it inherits the whole non-elidable-content discipline plus a mixed clean/dirty-page model — highest-complexity mmap row for zero named demand; error at map time, SIGBUS->abort, munmap on drop.
members.platform_risk: COW fault cost and overcommit behavior differ (Linux overcommit heuristics vs macOS compressor); 16K vs 4K pages change dirty-amplification by 4x.
members.trigger_if_deferred: A named scenario (e.g. linker relocating a mapped input) where the pread-copy baseline is >=1.3x slower end-to-end or >=2x peak RSS on a pinned workload on Linux x86-64.

members.name: filemap.map_file_shared_rw
members.scenario_consumer: NONE
members.contract_sketch: Already a ratified v1 non-goal (restated, not relitigated): shared writable mappings make every store a cross-process visible effect with no failure channel and unfixable fact-forgery/ordering semantics; WAL durability path uses pwrite+fdatasync rows instead.
members.platform_risk: Would be severe (msync semantics, unified buffer cache differences); moot when cut.
members.trigger_if_deferred: N/A (cut; prior decision stands)

members.name: os.proc_spawn
members.scenario_consumer: compiler driver (spawning assembler/linker/sub-tools); build-tool scenarios in the 51-map
members.contract_sketch: spawn(program_path, argv, env = inherit|explicit_list, stdio each of {inherit|null|pipe|io_file_handle}) -> Child handle via posix_spawn-class primitive; NO pre-exec hook, no fork exposure; fails with error result (ENOENT/EACCES/EAGAIN); teardown = Child must be consumed by proc_wait (dropping an un-waited Child is a checker error, no silent zombie).
members.platform_risk: posix_spawn is a real syscall on macOS but libc fork/exec emulation on glibc — spawn latency differs ~10x under large RSS; supported file-actions/attr flags differ (setsid, chdir file-action is nonstandard on macOS) — keep the wired surface to argv/env/stdio only, which is portable.
members.trigger_if_deferred: N/A (kept)

members.name: os.proc_wait
members.scenario_consumer: compiler driver (needs child exit codes to gate pipeline steps)
members.contract_sketch: wait(Child) -> {exit_code(u8)} | {terminated_by_signal(n)} blocking, EINTR retried internally; consumes the Child handle (linear — exactly one wait, no double-reap); failure channel only for kernel-level ECHILD anomalies -> abort (unreachable if handle is linear); teardown: wait IS the teardown.
members.platform_risk: Low; wait-status encoding identical in practice, signal numbering differs for a few signals but row reports the raw number plus a portable enum for TERM/KILL/SEGV.
members.trigger_if_deferred: N/A (kept)

members.name: os.time
members.scenario_consumer: servers (timeouts/deadlines), databases (WAL timestamps), engines (frame pacing) — broadest consumer set in the map
members.contract_sketch: monotonic_ns() -> u64 never-decreasing since arbitrary epoch, and wall_ns() -> unix epoch ns; both infallible, vDSO/commpage cost (no syscall on hot path), no handle/teardown; monotonic PINNED to exclude-suspend semantics on both OSes.
members.platform_risk: MATERIAL: Linux CLOCK_MONOTONIC excludes suspend, macOS CLOCK_MONOTONIC includes it — implement via CLOCK_UPTIME_RAW on macOS to honor the pinned exclude-suspend contract, or the same timeout code behaves differently across dev and deploy; flag in the acceptance battery.
members.trigger_if_deferred: N/A (kept)

members.name: os.sleep
members.scenario_consumer: servers (retry/backoff), benchmark harnesses; condvar timed-wait does not cover the no-lock case
members.contract_sketch: sleep(duration) blocks at-least the requested monotonic duration (may oversleep, never wakes early); EINTR retried internally so it is infallible; no teardown.
members.platform_risk: macOS timer coalescing/App Nap can oversleep more aggressively than Linux; at-least contract absorbs it — note it in perf docs, not semantics.
members.trigger_if_deferred: N/A (kept)

members.name: os.env_get
members.scenario_consumer: servers/databases reading deployment config (PORT, data dir) — standard container-deployment input channel
members.contract_sketch: env_get(name) -> Option<owned bytes> (not guaranteed UTF-8); read-only and race-free BY CONSTRUCTION because no setenv row exists anywhere in the language (no unsafe escape, so environ is immutable post-start); infallible aside from the None case; no teardown.
members.platform_risk: None material.
members.trigger_if_deferred: N/A (kept)

members.name: os.env_set
members.scenario_consumer: NONE (child env is set explicitly through proc_spawn's env list, which covers the only legitimate use)
members.contract_sketch: Cut precisely to keep env_get race-free; setenv is process-global mutable state with documented UB-adjacent races in every libc.
members.platform_risk: Moot.
members.trigger_if_deferred: N/A (cut)

members.name: os.args
members.scenario_consumer: compilers and every CLI-shaped scenario in the map
members.contract_sketch: args() -> immutable snapshot slice of argv as byte strings (argv[0] included, no UTF-8 guarantee); infallible, captured at process start, no teardown.
members.platform_risk: None.
members.trigger_if_deferred: N/A (kept)

members.name: os.random_csprng
members.scenario_consumer: hash-map seed (hashbrown-class DoS resistance — direct dependency of the kept container work), servers (tokens), databases (WAL/journal salts)
members.contract_sketch: fill_random(&uniq buf) fills the whole buffer with getentropy-class CSPRNG bytes, looping internally over the 256-byte-per-call cap; unrecoverable entropy failure -> abort (fail-closed, no weak-random fallback channel); no teardown; NOT a fact channel — output carries no optimizer-visible facts.
members.platform_risk: getentropy exists on both (macOS >=10.12, glibc >=2.25); 256-byte cap identical; low.
members.trigger_if_deferred: N/A (kept)

members.name: os.exit
members.scenario_consumer: compilers (exit codes are the driver protocol); every CLI scenario
members.contract_sketch: exit(code: u8) -> ! flushes the sealed stdio sinks then terminates without unwinding or user-level cleanup (drop glue does NOT run — documented); infallible; process end is the teardown.
members.platform_risk: None.
members.trigger_if_deferred: N/A (kept)

members.name: os.abort
members.scenario_consumer: already consumed by trap semantics (trap = process abort, ratified)
members.contract_sketch: Not a new row: user-callable abort is the already-decided trap mechanism spelled once; -> !, no failure channel, no cleanup.
members.platform_risk: None.
members.trigger_if_deferred: N/A (merged into trap)

members.name: os.signal_term_flag
members.scenario_consumer: server graceful shutdown (drain connections, fdatasync WAL, exit) — required by the server scenarios; not implementable from primitives since sigaction is unreachable without an escape hatch
members.contract_sketch: termination_requested() -> bool polling a sticky flag set by a runtime-installed SIGTERM/SIGINT handler that only stores to that flag (zero user code in signal context, so the entire async-signal-safety swamp stays sealed); install-once at startup, infallible, no teardown; startup also sets SIGPIPE to ignore so io-file write reports EPIPE as an ordinary error result.
members.platform_risk: sigaction semantics equivalent for this shape on both OSes; low. SIGPIPE-ignore is needed identically on both for server sockets/pipes.
members.trigger_if_deferred: N/A (kept)

members.name: os.signals_general
members.scenario_consumer: NONE (no v1 scenario needs a disposition beyond TERM/INT-as-flag and PIPE-as-ignore)
members.contract_sketch: Would be user-visible handlers or a signal-subscription queue; deferred because arbitrary handlers reintroduce reentrancy and async-signal-safety obligations that contradict the sealed-trust budget; if ever built, only the flag/queue shape (no user code in handler context) is admissible.
members.platform_risk: Signal numbering/realtime-signal availability differ (Linux SIGRTMIN.. absent on macOS); another reason to defer.
members.trigger_if_deferred: First named scenario requiring a signal outside {TERM, INT, PIPE} — e.g. SIGHUP config reload for a server — where a polled flag or an external control channel (socket/file) is shown inadequate; reinstate as additional sticky flags only, never user handlers.

members.name: os.cwd_get
members.scenario_consumer: NONE (io-file open passes paths to the kernel, which resolves relative paths against cwd for free; no v1 scenario must materialize the cwd string)
members.contract_sketch: Would be: cwd() -> owned path bytes, error on unlinked-cwd edge cases; deferred, and if reinstated it is read-only (see chdir cut) so it is race-free.
members.platform_risk: Low (PATH_MAX handling differs slightly).
members.trigger_if_deferred: First scenario that must emit absolute paths into artifacts (compiler dep-files/diagnostics) where requiring absolute-path inputs at the CLI boundary is demonstrated to break an existing consumer's invocation protocol.

members.name: os.chdir
members.scenario_consumer: NONE
members.contract_sketch: Cut: process-global mutable state that silently changes the meaning of every concurrent relative open — reintroduces exactly the cross-thread interference class the language exists to exclude; pass absolute or deliberately-relative paths to io-file instead.
members.platform_risk: Moot.
members.trigger_if_deferred: N/A (cut; a future openat/dir-handle row is the admissible replacement if per-directory scoping is ever demanded)

members.name: os.path_manipulation
members.scenario_consumer: NONE as a sealed row (compilers consume it, but from a checked library)
members.contract_sketch: join/basename/extension are pure byte-string computation — ordinary users implement them at par performance from primitives, so D16 excludes them from the sealed set outright; belongs in a checked library with zero trust budget.
members.platform_risk: None (both targets are POSIX; no drive letters).
members.trigger_if_deferred: N/A (cut by D16 first clause — implementable at par)

members.name: os.stdio_handles
members.scenario_consumer: compilers (diagnostics to stderr), every CLI/server scenario (logs)
members.contract_sketch: Not its own I/O row: stdout/stderr/stdin are the three pre-opened io-file handles (fd 0/1/2) exposed by accessors, reusing io-file's existing write/read rows verbatim (same error channel: short-write/EPIPE-as-error, EINTR retried); unbuffered at the sealed layer — line/block buffering is a checked library; process end is the teardown, exit flushes the sealed sinks.
members.platform_risk: None material; pipe vs tty vs file behavior identical across targets at this layer.
members.trigger_if_deferred: N/A (merged into io-file)

findings.detail: No unit owns filesystem metadata/namespace: stat/fstat (size, mtime, file-type), readdir, and mkdir appear nowhere in the three audits or in the pre-decided io-file list — not KEEP, not CUT, not DEFER-with-trigger. This breaks two of the five canonical programs outright: a build tool cannot do mtime-based staleness (full content re-hash of every input each run is not at par with make/ninja) and cannot create output directories; recursive parallel grep cannot walk a directory tree. It also punctures a kept row's own contract: map_file_ro(handle, offset, len) has no source for len — the one trusted fact of the row — so the read-a-big-index consumer cannot learn its index file's length without an O(size) read probe. Under this framework, absence of a syscall-rooted capability must be a priced verdict with a trigger; silence is not DEFER. A filesystem-namespace unit audit is required before ratification.

unlink and ftruncate are likewise unenumerated, hiding behind the pre-decided phrase 'plus what wal needs'. KV-store compaction cannot reclaim dead SSTable/WAL-segment space with only rename (rename-over frees at most one inode into another live name; no truncate row is stated for recycling), so disk usage grows monotonically — a correctness failure of the workload, not a perf shortfall. Either pin unlink/ftruncate into the io-file list explicitly before wal ratification or give each a DEFER trigger.

The accept-loop teardown contract is Linux-only lore and internally untyped. tcp_listen's 'shutdown-then-close ordering' for waking blocked acceptors requires shutdown() on a listening socket — an operation the kept net_shutdown row types only on tcp_stream, and which on macOS fails ENOTCONN without waking accept (on Linux it succeeds and wakes accept with an EINVAL-class return). close() is correctly documented as unblocking no one. Net effect: there is no portable, specified way for the graceful-shutdown path (signal_term_flag drain) to release a thread parked in accept; the sockopt row also never states whether SO_RCVTIMEO applies to accept on the listener (socket(7) says yes on Linux; macOS must be pinned), which is the only realistic portable poll mechanism. As specified, server drain is untestable on the dev machine and unspecified on the deploy target.

signal_term_flag's startup sets SIGPIPE to SIG_IGN process-wide, and ignored dispositions survive execve — so every proc_spawn child (assemblers, linkers, shell tools in the compiler-driver scenario) inherits SIGPIPE=IGN. Standard Unix tools in head-terminated pipelines rely on SIGPIPE death; inherited-IGN children instead see EPIPE and can error-spew or loop (a well-documented real-world bug class). proc_spawn's contract must reset SIGPIPE to SIG_DFL in the child (POSIX_SPAWN_SETSIGDEF) — currently unstated.

Child teardown is understated: proc_wait is the only consumer of the linear Child and blocks indefinitely; there is no kill/terminate row and no timed wait anywhere. A hung child pins a build worker forever, and on SIGTERM/Ctrl-C the parent's only exit is os.exit(), orphaning running children — not at par with make's interrupt behavior. Child termination/deadline needs an explicit verdict (KEEP or DEFER with a quantified trigger), not omission.

worker_index() is defined only for pool workers ('usize < pool size'), but the row's own named consumer — 'servers: per-worker state via worker-indexed arrays' — runs on the net unit's mandated v1 architecture: thread-per-connection scoped spawns, unbounded in count and off-pool, where worker_index has no defined value. The row is 'what makes the TLS cut viable', yet its replacement story does not cover its headline consumer. Define off-pool behavior (error? scope-scoped indices?) or re-scope the TLS-cut reinstatement trigger to include the thread-per-conn case.

net_send's KEEP-vs-merge rationale is voided by another kept row: os.signal_term_flag's startup ignores SIGPIPE process-wide, after which plain write(2) on a socket returns EPIPE on both platforms — exactly the io write row's stated EPIPE-as-error contract. The stated reason net_send cannot merge ('a merged write row could not hide the SIGPIPE divergence') is therefore false in context; recv merged but send did not, spending a full sealed-row battery on a redundant mechanism. Either merge net_send into the io write row (keeping net_sendv as the sole socket-specific emit row) or re-justify with a reason that survives global SIGPIPE-ignore (e.g., refusing to depend on runtime-startup ordering — then say exactly that).

os.exit 'flushes the sealed stdio sinks' contradicts stdio_handles' 'unbuffered at the sealed layer' — an unbuffered sink has nothing to flush. Meanwhile real buffering lives in a checked library whose drop glue exit() explicitly skips, so any program using buffered stdout (compilers, the named stdio consumer) that ends via exit() silently truncates diagnostics/output. Resolve the textual contradiction and define the exit/buffered-library composition (mandatory explicit flush before exit, or a registered sealed-sink flush).

sleep is kept twice — threads-par 'sleep' and filemap-os 'os.sleep' — two rows, two batteries, one capability, violating the one-spelling rule. Their rationales also contradict: threads-par says demote to condvar-timed-wait sugar if that ships; os.sleep asserts timed wait does not cover the no-lock case. Merge to a single row and settle whether the ratified condvar surface includes timed wait.

proc_spawn's platform note is factually stale in a decision-relevant direction: modern glibc posix_spawn is not 'fork/exec emulation' — since glibc 2.24 it uses clone(CLONE_VM|CLONE_VFORK) and copies no page tables, so the claimed ~10x large-RSS spawn-latency penalty on Linux is wrong. Correct it before it distorts any design or benchmarking decision.

sendfile-class zero-copy transmit was never enumerated — not even as DEFER with a trigger. The static-file-server workload pays one extra copy (map_file_ro + net_send) versus a sendfile baseline. Likely acceptable at v1 scale, but under this framework absence must be a priced verdict: add a DEFER row with a measured-copy/throughput trigger on the Linux deploy target.

tcp_connect has no caller deadline and OS SYN timeouts are minutes-long (~75s macOS, ~2min Linux); the row's named DB-client consumer hanging minutes per dead endpoint is not at par with production clients, and SO_SNDTIMEO does not bound connect. The gap implicitly rides the readiness deferral — make connect-deadline an explicit named clause of that trigger.

par.reduce's MERGE plan requires each chunk body to address a preallocated per-chunk &uniq output slot, but the for_chunks contract sketch passes only the input chunk — no chunk index and no zipped second disjoint output split. The merge verdict depends on an unstated for_chunks capability; state it in the for_chunks contract or the library form cannot be written as sketched.

scoped spawn specs one explicit default stack size but exposes no per-spawn stack knob (pool has one). Deep-recursion consumers — compiler frontends on scoped threads — need either a documented-generous default enforced at creation or a knob; the macOS 512KiB platform default makes this a real dev-machine trap if the spec'd default is not actually applied on every spawn path.

map_file_ro's SIGBUS-to-abort on external truncation is honest for process-owned DB index files, but if the static-file-server pattern maps request-addressable files, an external truncation becomes a remotely-triggerable whole-process abort. Add a consumer-scope note: server-facing file serving should use read-copy unless the served files are process-owned and immutable.

The UDP rows' consumers ('engines' realtime channels, telemetry, DNS-shaped services') are the loosest in the unit — none pinned to a numbered 51-map scenario the way D16's second prong demands. The keep is probably right (the deferred stub-resolver path also consumes them), but tighten the scenario naming before ratification.

Durability visibility gap: nothing in the audited units restates that Darwin fsync/fdatasync do not flush the drive cache (F_FULLFSYNC is the durable spelling), while the Chase-Lev row explicitly directs primary adversarial testing to the macOS dev machine. WAL-durability tests run on the dev box validate nothing unless the pre-ratified io rows pin the F_FULLFSYNC mapping — add the cross-reference so the acceptance battery cannot produce a false green on the platform where the semantics differ most.

five_programs_walk: Static-file web server: tcp_listen(reuseaddr) -> tcp_accept on shared<tcp_listener> -> scoped spawn per connection inside a long-lived scope (spawn EAGAIN gives a 503/close path) -> sockopt {TCP_NODELAY, SO_RCVTIMEO, SO_SNDTIMEO} -> request bytes via the merged io read row -> path join/sanitize as a pure checked library -> io-file open -> response via net_sendv (headers+body) with partial-write resume, or map_file_ro + net_send -> net_shutdown(WR) half-close -> drop-close -> signal_term_flag + per-stream RCVTIMEO for handler drain -> os.exit. BREAKS: (a) no fstat, so Content-Length and map_file_ro's len have no source except reading the whole file first — kills the mmap fast path (chunked encoding is a protocol downgrade, not par); (b) graceful drain cannot portably wake the thread blocked in accept (shutdown-on-listener is ENOTCONN on macOS, net_shutdown is not typed on listeners) — as specified, the accept thread parks forever and only a cleanup-skipping exit() escapes; (c) minor: one extra copy vs sendfile, and SIGBUS-abort exposure if serving from maps that others can truncate. Writable, but not at par on the metadata path, and shutdown is broken-as-specified on the dev platform.

Build tool: args + env_get -> manifest via io read -> dependency graph in checked containers -> N scoped worker threads + conc_queue (mpmc) of ready jobs — correctly avoiding par fork-join since jobs block, so the no-blocking card and the deferred blocking-hatch are mutually consistent here -> proc_spawn(argv, explicit env, stdio=pipe) -> pipe drain on scoped threads -> proc_wait exit codes gate dependents -> outputs published via write + fsync + rename + dir-fsync -> diagnostics on merged stderr -> os.exit code. BREAKS FATAL: no stat/mtime means incremental builds are impossible at par (full content re-hash of the tree every run); no mkdir means output directories cannot be created at all; no unlink means no clean step; no child kill means Ctrl-C orphans running compilers; spawned tools inherit SIGPIPE=IGN and can misbehave in pipelines. The scheduling/execution core is fully served by kept rows; the unowned filesystem-metadata seam is what breaks the program.

LZ4 CLI: args -> input via io-file open + read loop (or the stdin fd0 accessor) into an owned growable buffer -> pool(n=0 default cores) -> par.for_chunks over frame-sized blocks for parallel compression (checker-proved disjoint &uniq chunks are exactly the needed shape; compression itself is pure checked code) -> checksums via the merged par.reduce library form (wrapping-int monoid, order-insensitive) -> output via io write with short-write loop -> exit(0/1). PASSES at par with only the kept rows. Residuals: no fstat means no input-size preallocation (streaming append is fine and par holds), and the reduce library needs a chunk-index/output-slot facility that the for_chunks sketch does not yet state.

KV store with WAL: serving via tcp_listen/tcp_accept, thread-per-conn scoped spawns with SO_RCVTIMEO deadlines, net_send/net_sendv responses, net_shutdown(RD) as the cross-thread cancel; state via shared<T> + mutex + publish-snapshot-cell memtable and conc_queue to background flush/compaction threads; random_csprng for hash seeds; durability via pwrite + fdatasync WAL, checkpoint = write-new + fsync + rename + dir-fsync (all pre-decided io rows); recovery reads the WAL to EOF via a pread loop (read 0 = EOF), so the missing stat is survivable there; SSTable reads via map_file_ro; shutdown via signal_term_flag -> drain -> final fdatasync -> exit. BREAKS: compaction cannot reclaim space — no unlink/ftruncate anywhere, so disk usage grows without bound (a workload failure, not a perf shortfall); map_file_ro len again needs fstat or an O(file) startup probe; the blocked-accept wakeup hole is shared with the web server; and dev-machine durability testing is void without an F_FULLFSYNC pin in the io rows. Everything else runs at par on kept rows.

Parallel grep: args (pattern + explicit file list) -> io-file open per file -> read loop into a buffer (the mmap path is blocked by the fstat-len gap, but read-based scanning is GNU grep's own approach, so par holds) -> pool + conc_queue (mpmc) of files, par.for_chunks within large files with chunk-boundary straddle handling in the pure matcher -> matches serialized to stdout under mutex via the merged stdio write row -> exit code from a shared counter. BREAKS FATAL in its normal form: recursive grep over a directory tree is unwritable — no readdir and no stat to classify entries. Fed an external file list (find | xargs style) it passes at par; early-exit modes (-q/-l) over-scan only within one chunk, since cooperative cancellation via publish-snapshot-cell between chunks is available — acceptable.
attack.overall: The row-level verdicts inside the three audited units are mostly sound: none of the CUT/DEFER calls (detached spawn, TLS-the-protocol, TLS-storage, yield, barrier, cancellation, readiness, DNS, madvise, private-rw maps) breaks any of the five canonical programs, and several rows are exemplary — udp_recvfrom's intersection contract, os.time's exclude-suspend pin, the TLS cut by D16's first prong, the net_recv merge. What fails is the seam no unit owns: filesystem metadata and namespace (stat/fstat, readdir, mkdir, unlink, ftruncate) was never enumerated anywhere — neither kept nor cut nor deferred with a trigger — and that single omission breaks the build tool and recursive grep outright, starves the KV store's compaction, and leaves map_file_ro's own len parameter unsatisfiable: one FATAL. Second-order, the units were audited in isolation and contradict each other where they compose: SIGPIPE is solved twice and the duplication voids net_send's non-merge rationale; sleep is kept twice with contradictory demotion logic; exit-flushes-sinks contradicts unbuffered-sealed-stdio; worker_index's server consumer runs off-pool where the row is undefined; and tcp_listen's teardown cites a shutdown operation that net_shutdown does not type and macOS does not honor. Process teardown is the third weak axis: no child kill or timed wait, SIGPIPE=IGN inheritance across exec, and no portable accept wakeup for graceful drain. Recommended before ratification: (1) a filesystem-namespace unit audit with explicit verdicts and quantified triggers; (2) a cross-unit composition pass that walks exactly these five programs against contract text rather than intent; (3) pinned teardown contracts for child processes and blocked acceptors with dev-vs-deploy divergence stated, plus the F_FULLFSYNC cross-reference so dev-machine durability tests cannot go falsely green. No audited KEEP needs to flip to CUT; two KEEPs (net_send, one of the two sleep rows) should become MERGEs, and the omitted rows must be adjudicated before any wal or server-scenario ratification proceeds.

final_members.name: scoped spawn/join (region-typed)
final_members.scenario_consumer: server request handling (thread-per-connection workers over conc_queue); compilers' parallel pipeline stages
final_members.contract_sketch: scope(|s| s.spawn(closure borrowing region-scoped &/&uniq data)); spawn fails err(EAGAIN) before the child ever runs; AMENDED (resolves stack-knob minor): spawn takes optional stack_bytes, and the spec'd explicit default (8 MiB, stated in the spec, never platform-inherited) is applied on every spawn path; child trap = process abort; scope exit blocks until every child is joined, so no borrow outlives its region.
final_members.platform_note: Explicit default stack applied on both OSes so macOS's ~512KiB pthread default can never leak in as a dev-machine deep-recursion trap; clone vs pthread_create stays behind the seal.

final_members.name: unscoped/detached spawn
final_members.scenario_consumer: NONE
final_members.contract_sketch: Would be a 'static-env, never-joined, leaked-at-exit thread — exactly the teardown ambiguity scoped spawn forbids. Reinstate only if a v1 scenario exhibits a thread lifetime provably un-nestable in any enclosing scope; program-lifetime scope + conc_queue covers all 51 scenarios at par.
final_members.platform_note: Cut avoids exit-time race divergence (macOS dyld vs glibc atexit/TLS teardown ordering).

final_members.name: worker-pool knob: thread count + stack size
final_members.scenario_consumer: server request handling (pool sizing); codec parallel validation (size = physical cores)
final_members.contract_sketch: pool(n, stack_bytes) -> pool | err; n=0 means available_parallelism (trusted sysconf/sysctl read, unreachable from primitives); teardown: pool drop drains queue then joins all workers.
final_members.platform_note: available_parallelism source differs (sysctl hw.* vs sched_getaffinity); Apple Silicon P/E asymmetry makes 'core count' ambiguous — spec which count each platform returns.

final_members.name: worker-pool knobs: pinning/affinity/priorities
final_members.scenario_consumer: NONE in v1 (engines plausible future consumer)
final_members.contract_sketch: Would be: pin worker i to cpu set / sched class at pool construction; err on EPERM/unsupported; no runtime re-pinning.
final_members.platform_note: HIGH divergence: macOS thread_affinity_policy is a hint ignored on Apple Silicon (untestable on dev machine); SCHED_FIFO needs CAP_SYS_NICE on Linux.

final_members.name: par.for_chunks
final_members.scenario_consumer: codecs: parallel validation, partition pass of parallel sort; also carrier for the par.reduce library form
final_members.contract_sketch: AMENDED (resolves reduce-dependency minor): body receives (chunk_index: usize, in_chunk: &uniq [T], and optionally a zipped out-slot &uniq U drawn from a checker-split disjoint output array with the same partition); checker-proved disjointness carried across the thread boundary is the nonforgeable fact users cannot build; returns only after all chunks complete; any trap aborts the process; no early-exit channel in v1.
final_members.platform_note: None material; chunk-size heuristics are tuning, not semantics. The new indexed/zipped out-slot shape is added checker surface and needs its own hostile fact-channel review (see open flags).

final_members.name: par fork-join (recursive task spawn)
final_members.scenario_consumer: build-system-style fork-join (compilers); recursive parallel sort (codecs)
final_members.contract_sketch: Inside a par scope: h = s.fork(task); v = h.join() returns the task's value; work-stealing internal; trap in any task aborts the process; par scope exit implies every forked task completed.
final_members.platform_note: Correctness rests on internal deque memory orderings — arm64 (dev machine) is the honest stress bed; x86-64 TSO can mask fence bugs, so deploy-target green is insufficient evidence.

final_members.name: par.reduce (wrapping-int monoids only)
final_members.scenario_consumer: codec checksums (adler/CRC-style) — served by the merged form
final_members.contract_sketch: Checked-library helper over the AMENDED for_chunks: chunk i writes its partial into out-slot i of a preallocated disjoint output array (now expressible — for_chunks passes chunk_index and a zipped &uniq out-slot), then a serial O(chunks) fold; wrapping-int monoids make combine order-insensitive, so at par with a fused builtin; zero new trusted LOC. Promote to sealed only if a preregistered codec checksum bench shows the library form losing >=3% to a fused reduce.
final_members.platform_note: None — inherits for_chunks.

final_members.name: thread-local storage
final_members.scenario_consumer: NONE user-visible (allocator TLS is runtime-internal)
final_members.contract_sketch: Replacement AMENDED to cover the off-pool consumer (resolves worker_index major): pool code uses worker_index()-addressed arrays; thread-per-connection servers pass a slot index into the closure at spawn, recycled via a checked-library free list — both at par (one indexed load). Reinstatement trigger RE-SCOPED: >=5% measured overhead vs native TLS in EITHER the pool-indexed OR the thread-per-conn slot-indexed case, or detached spawn ever reinstated.
final_members.platform_note: Cut avoids TLS destructor-ordering and dlopen divergence between macOS dyld and glibc — a large verification cost avoided.

final_members.name: sleep (threads-par spelling)
final_members.scenario_consumer: same consumers, served by the single os.sleep row
final_members.contract_sketch: Row deleted; os.sleep is the one spelling (resolves the duplicate-row minor). Ruling settled here: os.sleep stays sealed even if the condvar surface ships timed wait — timed wait does not cover the no-lock case, so the threads-par audit's demote-to-sugar note is overruled.
final_members.platform_note: n/a — see os.sleep.

final_members.name: yield
final_members.scenario_consumer: NONE — spin backoff lives inside the sealed mutex and internal deque
final_members.contract_sketch: sched_yield semantics are unspecified and invite user spin loops competing with kept sync members. Reinstate only if a user-level wait loop beats kept sync primitives by >=5% and needs yield for fairness — expected never.
final_members.platform_note: sched_yield behavior differs across kernels/schedulers — precisely why it stays internal.

final_members.name: thread id / pool worker index
final_members.scenario_consumer: engines/codecs: per-worker state on the pool via worker-indexed arrays; servers get per-thread state via spawn-time indices (pure user code, no row needed)
final_members.contract_sketch: AMENDED (resolves off-pool major): worker_index() -> usize < pool_size is typed as available ONLY inside pool task bodies (for_chunks/fork-join) — off-pool use is a checker-rejected context, not a runtime error; scoped thread-per-conn code passes an explicit index into the closure at spawn instead. thread id = opaque u64 unique for process lifetime (synthesized, not gettid); no failure channel; ~trivial trusted LOC.
final_members.platform_note: None — ids synthesized in the runtime, not read from the OS.

final_members.name: thread naming
final_members.scenario_consumer: NONE (debuggability nicety, no performance claim)
final_members.contract_sketch: Would be: set_name at spawn, best-effort, truncated, no error channel.
final_members.platform_note: pthread_setname_np diverges: macOS self-only 1-arg, Linux 2-arg with 15-byte cap — a different contract per platform for zero scenario value.

final_members.name: panic-across-thread semantics
final_members.scenario_consumer: every concurrent scenario (semantic rule, zero new trusted LOC)
final_members.contract_sketch: Trap on any thread = immediate whole-process abort; no cross-thread unwinding, no lock poisoning, no observation channel — join never reports 'child trapped' because the process is already gone.
final_members.platform_note: None — abort delivery identical on both platforms.

final_members.name: scheduler internals (Chase-Lev deque)
final_members.scenario_consumer: internal-only: implements fork-join and for_chunks scheduling; no D16 sealed-member row spent
final_members.contract_sketch: Per-worker deque, owner push/pop, thief steal; no public API; pool shutdown drains queues before joining workers; carries its own memory-ordering proof through the acceptance battery (it is trusted LOC).
final_members.platform_note: Classic Chase-Lev fence bugs are invisible on x86-64 TSO and manifest on arm64 — run adversarial concurrency tests on the arm64 dev machine as the primary bed.

final_members.name: blocking-call integration (escape hatch for IO in par bodies)
final_members.scenario_consumer: NONE in v1 — card rule stands: no blocking calls in par bodies; blocking IO lives on scoped threads feeding the pool via conc_queue
final_members.contract_sketch: Would be: block_in_place-style hatch retiring the current worker and spawning a replacement; err on spawn failure; replacement joins at pool drop.
final_members.platform_note: None material; the hatch itself is platform-neutral.

final_members.name: barrier/latch primitives
final_members.scenario_consumer: NONE as sealed members — engine frame sync served by a checked-library barrier over kept counter+mutex+condvar at par (waits are microsecond-to-millisecond scale)
final_members.contract_sketch: Library form: latch = counter with condvar wake at zero; barrier = two-phase latch; zero new trusted LOC. Reinstate as sealed if a preregistered engine scenario measures >=5% frame-time regression vs a native futex barrier on the deploy target.
final_members.platform_note: None — inherits the sync members' already-adjudicated futex/ulock divergence.

final_members.name: scope cancellation
final_members.scenario_consumer: NONE — server request-timeout served by cooperative flag polling via publish-snapshot-cell, at par, zero trusted LOC; blocked-in-IO release served by net_shutdown(RD) and timed accept
final_members.contract_sketch: Cooperative library form: shared cancel flag checked at chunk/task boundaries; forced cancellation of a blocked thread rides the evring reinstatement trigger — adjudicate together with evring, never before.
final_members.platform_note: Cut avoids the pthread_cancel/signal cancellation-point swamp (macOS vs Linux).

final_members.name: tcp_connect
final_members.scenario_consumer: network sockets, TCP client leg: DB clients, servers dialing upstreams (51-map request-path scenarios)
final_members.contract_sketch: socket()+connect() merged: blocking connect to (ip,port) -> linear tcp_stream or errno-class error (ECONNREFUSED/ETIMEDOUT/ENETUNREACH); OS-default SYN timeout; AMENDED: the missing caller deadline is now an explicit named clause of the readiness_wait defer trigger (minutes-long SYN timeout is documented as not-at-par for production clients until that trigger fires); teardown = close via handle drop.
final_members.platform_note: Low. SIGPIPE handled by the runtime's unconditional startup SIGPIPE=IGN invariant (Darwin SO_NOSIGPIPE may still be set defensively); SOCK_CLOEXEC atomic on Linux, fcntl window on macOS (dev-only exposure).

final_members.name: tcp_listen
final_members.scenario_consumer: network sockets: server accept side (51-map server scenarios)
final_members.contract_sketch: socket()+bind()+listen() merged: (ip,port,backlog,reuseaddr) -> linear tcp_listener or errno error (EADDRINUSE/EACCES). AMENDED teardown (resolves accept-drain major): the unportable 'shutdown-then-close' clause is DELETED (shutdown on a listener is ENOTCONN on macOS); close frees the fd and wakes no blocked acceptor; the specified graceful-drain mechanism is timed accept — SO_RCVTIMEO typed on the listener via sockopt_set — polled against termination_requested().
final_members.platform_note: Backlog clamping differs (somaxconn); SO_REUSEADDR laxer on macOS — Linux (deploy) semantics normative. Darwin accept-honors-SO_RCVTIMEO must be verified in the battery (see open flags); fallback is the loopback self-connect wake pattern in the card, zero new rows.

final_members.name: tcp_accept
final_members.scenario_consumer: network sockets: thread-per-connection server loop (the v1 server architecture with evring deferred)
final_members.contract_sketch: Blocking accept on shared<tcp_listener> -> (linear tcp_stream, peer_addr) or errno error; ECONNABORTED retried internally, EMFILE/ENFILE surfaced; AMENDED: when the listener's SO_RCVTIMEO fires, accept returns a timeout error value — this is the specified portable drain/poll mechanism; per-stream teardown independent of listener.
final_members.platform_note: accept4(SOCK_CLOEXEC) on Linux vs accept+fcntl on macOS; Darwin inherited-O_NONBLOCK quirk hidden inside the row; Linux accept-honors-SO_RCVTIMEO is documented, Darwin pinned by battery test.

final_members.name: socket_raw / bind_raw / listen_raw
final_members.scenario_consumer: NONE standalone — every consumer wants the composed handle
final_members.contract_sketch: Folded into tcp_connect, tcp_listen, udp_bind; a bare fd with no role has no checked contract, so it never exists as a value. Reinstate only if a socket type outside {TCP, UDP} is admitted (e.g. unix-domain), and then as its own composed row.
final_members.platform_note: None (removed surface).

final_members.name: net_recv (stream read)
final_members.scenario_consumer: network sockets: request parsing in thread-per-conn servers
final_members.contract_sketch: Merged into the kept io-file read row: blocking read on fd-backed byte stream -> 0..n bytes, 0 = EOF/peer-FIN, EINTR per the io row's policy, ECONNRESET as errno error, SO_RCVTIMEO expiry mapped to a timeout error value; one read row, one contract, two handle kinds. Split back only if the io read row's verified contract proves incompatible with the timeout channel during the battery.
final_members.platform_note: Low — read(2) is the portable part of sockets; only the errno set widens.

final_members.name: net_send (stream write)
final_members.scenario_consumer: network sockets: response emission — served by the io-file write row on socket handles
final_members.contract_sketch: FLIPPED KEEP->MERGE (resolves voided-rationale major): with the runtime's unconditional startup SIGPIPE=IGN invariant (see os.signal_term_flag), plain write(2) on a socket returns EPIPE-as-error on both platforms — exactly the io write row's contract, so the audited non-merge reason ('a merged row could not hide the SIGPIPE divergence') is false in context. Socket handles widen the io write row's errno set (EPIPE/ECONNRESET; SO_SNDTIMEO expiry -> timeout error); partial-write count contract identical; teardown = shutdown(WR)+close.
final_members.platform_note: The merge depends on SIGPIPE=IGN being a sealed, unconditional runtime-startup invariant, not opt-in — pinned in the runtime-startup spec; the io write row's battery must exercise socket handles (see open flags).

final_members.name: net_sendv (writev gather)
final_members.scenario_consumer: network sockets: header+body response assembly; par unreachable without it (extra copy or two syscalls interacting with Nagle)
final_members.contract_sketch: Sole socket-specific emit row after the net_send merge. AMENDED: plain writev on both platforms under the startup SIGPIPE=IGN invariant (MSG_NOSIGNAL branch deleted); gather-send with partial-write contract (total bytes consumed across iovecs); errno channel identical to the merged write row. readv counterpart stays out; trigger = a codec/server scenario showing a measured receive-path copy >5% of request cost.
final_members.platform_note: Low. IOV_MAX 1024 on both in practice.

final_members.name: net_shutdown
final_members.scenario_consumer: network sockets: half-close for protocol EOF; the blocking-world stream-cancel primitive — shutdown(RD) from a sibling releases a thread parked in blocking read
final_members.contract_sketch: AMENDED typing made explicit: shutdown(how in {RD,WR,RDWR}) on shared<tcp_stream> ONLY — not typed on listeners (listener drain is the timed-accept contract instead); releases blocked readers/writers with EOF/EPIPE; ENOTCONN after peer-close defined as success; does not free the fd — teardown remains close.
final_members.platform_note: macOS wakes racing readers with 0/EOF where Linux may surface ENOTCONN — contract normalizes both to EOF.

final_members.name: net_close
final_members.scenario_consumer: all socket consumers
final_members.contract_sketch: Merged into the kept io close/handle-drop row: linear-handle consumption, close(2) once, EINTR-on-close policy inherited (fd dead either way); SO_LINGER deliberately unexposed. Split out only if a kept protocol scenario's correctness requires drained close.
final_members.platform_note: Low; EINTR-on-close divergence already owned by the io close row.

final_members.name: sockopt_set (closed enum: TCP_NODELAY, SO_REUSEADDR, SO_RCVBUF, SO_SNDBUF, SO_RCVTIMEO, SO_SNDTIMEO)
final_members.scenario_consumer: network sockets: NODELAY for latency par; REUSEADDR for restart; buffer sizes for throughput par; RCV/SND timeouts load-bearing for thread-per-conn deadlines; AMENDED: listener SO_RCVTIMEO is the graceful-drain mechanism
final_members.contract_sketch: Typed setter per enum member (no generic setsockopt, no getsockopt in v1); AMENDED typing: SO_RCVTIMEO is additionally typed on tcp_listener, where it bounds accept (timeout error value) — the drain path for signal_term_flag shutdown; kernel-clamped values accepted silently per option contract; excluded by name: SO_REUSEPORT, SO_KEEPALIVE, SO_LINGER, TCP_CORK/NOPUSH (each with its audited trigger).
final_members.platform_note: MATERIAL on buffers: Linux doubles and clamps SO_RCVBUF/SNDBUF to rmem_max/wmem_max, Darwin does not — contract says 'hint, kernel-clamped', never 'exact'. Darwin accept+SO_RCVTIMEO is a mandatory battery item (open flag).

final_members.name: readiness_wait (kqueue/epoll)
final_members.scenario_consumer: readiness/completion multiplexing — already the deferred evring row's scenario; v1 servers are thread-per-connection
final_members.contract_sketch: If reinstated: register fd set, blocking wait -> ready subset, level-triggered only; deregister-on-close defined against the close row. Trigger AMENDED with a third clause covering connect deadlines (see deferred_with_triggers).
final_members.platform_note: HIGH divergence (kqueue vs epoll registration, edge/level defaults, closed-fd error reporting) — a portable sealed contract is real design work paid twice if evring later subsumes it; on firing, re-evaluate un-deferring evring first.

final_members.name: dns_resolve (getaddrinfo)
final_members.scenario_consumer: NONE at v1 — request-path scenarios connect to config-provided numeric endpoints
final_members.contract_sketch: Deferred with a 'resolve via OS' card: v1 takes numeric ip:port (address parsing is pure checked code); getaddrinfo drags NSS/dlopen/mDNSResponder into trusted surface and blocks unboundedly — worst battery cost in the unit.
final_members.platform_note: HIGH: macOS resolves via mDNSResponder, Linux via nsswitch/resolv.conf — same call, materially different behavior between dev and deploy.

final_members.name: tls
final_members.scenario_consumer: NONE — no v1 demand-map scenario names TLS; fails D16's first prong too
final_members.contract_sketch: TLS is pure computation over the kept stream rows — ordinary users CAN implement it from primitives + checked libraries, so it is a future checked library, never a sealed row. Reopen only as a checked library when a scenario names it; 'library crypto at par' is a CPU-intrinsics question filed against a future intrinsics unit.
final_members.platform_note: n/a (no row).

final_members.name: udp_bind
final_members.scenario_consumer: network sockets, UDP leg: engines' realtime channels, telemetry, DNS-shaped services — to be pinned to numbered 51-map scenarios before ratification (open flag); also consumed by the deferred stub-resolver path
final_members.contract_sketch: socket(DGRAM)+bind merged: (ip,port; 0 = ephemeral) -> linear udp_socket or errno error; optional connect(peer) variant for fixed-peer filtering + ECONNREFUSED delivery; teardown = close via handle drop.
final_members.platform_note: Low; ICMP-error surfacing on connected UDP reliable on Linux, best-effort on Darwin — contract marks it advisory.

final_members.name: udp_sendto
final_members.scenario_consumer: network sockets, UDP leg (as udp_bind; scenario pinning pending)
final_members.contract_sketch: Blocking sendto(buf, addr): whole datagram sent or errno error (EMSGSIZE oversize, EAGAIN on SO_SNDTIMEO) — never partial, by datagram semantics; no SIGPIPE channel; teardown shared with udp_socket.
final_members.platform_note: Low; max datagram size and default sndbuf differ — contract exposes EMSGSIZE rather than a constant.

final_members.name: udp_recvfrom
final_members.scenario_consumer: network sockets, UDP leg (as udp_bind; scenario pinning pending)
final_members.contract_sketch: Blocking recvfrom(buf) -> (n, peer_addr, truncated: bool) or errno error (EAGAIN on SO_RCVTIMEO); oversize datagram delivered truncated with flag set, remainder discarded — the flag is the portable contract; true-length-on-truncation reinstates only for a named consumer accepting a Linux-only note.
final_members.platform_note: MATERIAL, hence bool-only: Linux MSG_TRUNC reports true length, Darwin only flags — row exposes the intersection so dev and deploy behave identically.

final_members.name: sendfile-class zero-copy transmit
final_members.scenario_consumer: NONE named yet — static-file server is the plausible consumer; v1 pays one extra copy via map_file_ro/read + write
final_members.contract_sketch: NEW ROW (resolves unpriced-absence minor): would be transmit(io_file_handle range -> socket) with partial-progress contract; not built now — the extra copy is priced as acceptable at v1 scale.
final_members.platform_note: Darwin sendfile differs from Linux in signature and semantics — Linux-normative if reinstated; another reason to defer.

final_members.name: filemap.map_file_ro
final_members.scenario_consumer: read-a-big-index (database immutable index/SSTable; compiler large object/archive inputs)
final_members.contract_sketch: map(io_file_handle, offset, len) -> RoMap owning a fixed-length read-only byte view; ONLY the length is a trusted fact (fixed at map time); AMENDED: len now has a source — the new os.fstat row (fstat-then-map is the stated idiom), closing the audit's unsatisfiable-parameter hole; mapped bytes never margined, every content-derived check non-elidable; error result at map time; touching an externally truncated page = SIGBUS -> process abort; restricted to non-O_DIRECT handles; teardown = munmap on drop, infallible. AMENDED consumer-scope note: request-addressable served files must use read-copy — external truncation is a remotely-triggerable whole-process abort; maps are for process-owned immutable files only.
final_members.platform_note: 16KB pages on macOS arm64 vs 4KB on Linux x86-64 — rounding page-size-parametric, never hardcoded; SIGBUS-on-truncation aborts on both.

final_members.name: filemap.unmap
final_members.scenario_consumer: same as map_file_ro
final_members.contract_sketch: Not a separate row: unmap IS RoMap teardown (drop = munmap, infallible); no early-unmap-while-borrowed possible because the byte view borrows the RoMap.
final_members.platform_note: None beyond the map row.

final_members.name: filemap.madvise_subset
final_members.scenario_consumer: NONE (pure perf hint; read-a-big-index functionally complete without it)
final_members.contract_sketch: Would be: advise(RoMap, range, {sequential|willneed|dontneed-cold}), hint-only, infallible, no teardown — safe to defer because it cannot change observable behavior.
final_members.platform_note: High divergence: macOS lacks/reinterprets several Linux advices (MADV_FREE vs MADV_DONTNEED); hints tuned on Apple Silicon may be no-ops or pessimizations on Linux x86-64.

final_members.name: filemap.map_file_private_rw
final_members.scenario_consumer: NONE named in v1 (nearest: linker patch-in-place; pread-into-owned-buffer is the par baseline)
final_members.contract_sketch: Would be: COW private writable mapping; inherits the full non-elidable-content discipline plus a mixed clean/dirty-page model — highest-complexity mmap row for zero named demand.
final_members.platform_note: COW fault cost and overcommit behavior differ (Linux heuristics vs macOS compressor); 16K vs 4K pages change dirty amplification 4x.

final_members.name: filemap.map_file_shared_rw
final_members.scenario_consumer: NONE
final_members.contract_sketch: Ratified v1 non-goal restated: shared writable mappings make every store a cross-process visible effect with no failure channel and unfixable fact-forgery/ordering semantics; WAL durability uses pwrite+fdatasync rows.
final_members.platform_note: Would be severe (msync, unified buffer cache differences); moot when cut.

final_members.name: os.proc_spawn
final_members.scenario_consumer: compiler driver (assembler/linker/sub-tools); build-tool scenarios in the 51-map
